title: Greek Moussaka

subs.name: Zucchini (courgette) slices or thinly sliced potato can replace some or all of the brinjal; potato layers give a heartier, denser moussaka
For a vegetarian version, use finely chopped mushrooms and cooked green lentils (masoor dal) in equal parts; the cinnamon-tomato sauce suits both

ingredients.name: canned tomatoes or 3 ripe tomatoes, roughly chopped

subs.name: A small pinch of garam masala adds a warm spiced note in the same spirit, though the flavour profile will be distinctly more South Asian
Besan (chickpea flour) can replace maida in the béchamel for a gluten-free version; the sauce will have a slightly nuttier flavour

steps.text: Preheat the oven to 200°C. Lay the brinjal rounds on a baking tray, brush both sides with olive oil, and season with salt. Roast for 20 minutes, flipping once halfway, until the slices are soft, golden, and slightly collapsed. Set aside.

While the brinjal roasts, make the meat sauce. Heat 2 tablespoons of olive oil in a kadai over medium-high flame. Add the onion and cook for 5 minutes until soft, then add the garlic and cook for 1 minute. Add the keema and break it up with a spoon, cooking until it is browned all over and no pink remains.

Add the tomatoes, tomato paste, cinnamon, and paprika to the meat. Season with salt, stir well, and simmer uncovered for 15 minutes until the sauce is thick and the liquid has largely evaporated — the mixture should look like a moist, fragrant ragù rather than a soupy sauce.

Make the béchamel: melt the butter in a saucepan over medium heat, stir in the flour, and cook for 1 minute while stirring until the mixture smells biscuity. Gradually pour in the warm milk, a ladleful at a time, whisking constantly to prevent lumps. Keep whisking over medium heat for 5–7 minutes until the sauce thickens enough to coat the back of a spoon. Season with salt and nutmeg, remove from heat, and stir in the egg quickly.

Assemble in a deep baking dish (about 25×30 cm): lay half the roasted brinjal in a single layer across the base. Spread all the meat sauce over the brinjal, then add a second layer of the remaining brinjal rounds. Pour the béchamel evenly over the top, spreading it with a spoon to cover the surface completely.
Bake at 180°C for 35–40 minutes until the béchamel top is a deep golden-brown and the edges are bubbling. A skewer inserted in the centre should feel hot when touched to your wrist.

Remove from the oven and allow the moussaka to rest for at least 15 minutes before cutting — this resting time is essential for the layers to firm up so the slices hold their shape. Serve warm, not piping hot.

cross_contact_notes: Ingredient metadata cannot prove an allergen-free kitchen. Check packaged labels and cross-contact statements for your products.
safety_warnings: Keep raw animal products, their plates and utensils separate from cooked food; wash hands and surfaces after contact.
critical_checks: Use an appropriate food thermometer and safe doneness guidance for the specific cut of meat.
Use pasteurised eggs for preparations served raw or lightly cooked, especially for higher-risk people.
trust.provenance_summary: AI-assisted original draft; declared licence original.
trust.substitution_warning: A substitution can introduce allergens or change dietary suitability. Never claim a swap is safe without checking the replacement's label and the user's allergy context.

substitution_notes: keema → For a vegetarian version, use finely chopped mushrooms and cooked green lentils (masoor dal) in equal parts; the cinnamon-tomato sauce suits both; brinjal → Zucchini (courgette) slices or thinly sliced potato can replace some or all of the brinjal; potato layers give a heartier, denser moussaka; maida → Besan (chickpea flour) can replace maida in the béchamel for a gluten-free version; the sauce will have a slightly nuttier flavour; cinnamon → A small pinch of garam masala adds a warm spiced note in the same spirit, though the flavour profile will be distinctly more South Asian
indian_kitchen_adaptation: An OTG or countertop oven works perfectly for this recipe. For kitchens without an oven, the assembled moussaka can be slow-cooked on the stovetop: place the dish on a tawa over very low flame, cover with a tight lid, and cook for 35–40 minutes until the top sets (the béchamel won't brown, but the flavours will be identical). Brinjal (baingan) is identical to eggplant and widely available. Keema is sold at most meat counters. Maida is the same as the all-purpose flour in the béchamel — available everywhere in India.
